title: AD Lateral Movement — Attacker Setup (hosts, DNS, krb5, tooling)
body:
GROUP-MEMBERSHIP TRIAGE — this decides which technique you reach for:
  Remote Desktop Users    -> RDP            -> Remmina / xfreerdp3
  Remote Management Users -> WinRM          -> winrs, psremoting, evil-winrm
  Regular Domain Users    -> SSH            -> ssh
  Local Administrators    -> PTH / Over-PTH -> impacket-psexec, scshell,
                             (SCM/WMI/DCOM)    impacket-wmiexec, impacket-dcomexec
                             or Pass-the-Ticket
So: enumerate each compromised user's group memberships FIRST, then pick the
technique that group unlocks. Local admin is the jackpot (full code exec);
Remote Management Users gets you winrs/psremoting; RDP/SSH are interactive only.

Before any Kerberos-based lateral movement works from Kali, your box has to resolve domain names and speak Kerberos. Skipping this is why getTGT/wmiexec/psexec mysteriously fail with name or auth errors. Do this once at the start of the engagement. Two workflows run in parallel from here on: (A) from a compromised Windows client, (B) from Kali directly — if one stalls, switch to the other.
cmd:
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 1 — map the domain hosts into /etc/hosts
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Kerberos and many Impacket tools need FQDN -> IP resolution.
# vajra's ad_hostmap automates discovery + /etc/hosts mapping:
git clone https://github.com/jimi421/vajra.git
cd vajra/ad_hostsmap
chmod +x ad_hostmap.sh
./ad_hostmap.sh --auto          # auto-detects local /24, sweeps, maps
#   -> writes ad_hostmap-hosts.txt (IP list)
#   -> backs up /etc/hosts, adds DC1.corp.com / FILES04.corp.com / etc.
#   -> flags the likely DC and domain name

# Native one-liner alternative (NetExec writes the hosts file for you):
nxc smb ips.txt -u jen -p 'Nexus123!' -d corp.com --generate-hosts-file /tmp/hosts
#   then review and append /tmp/hosts to /etc/hosts

# Manual equivalent if you prefer:
#   sudo tee -a /etc/hosts <<EOF
#   192.168.x.70   DC1.corp.com corp.com DC1
#   192.168.x.73   FILES04.corp.com FILES04
#   EOF

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 2 — point DNS at the Domain Controller
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
sudo vim /etc/resolv.conf
#   add as a nameserver line (DC is the domain's DNS server):
#     nameserver 192.168.x.70
# Keep your existing resolvers below it for internet.

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 3 — install Kerberos client (for -k / ccache workflows)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
sudo apt install krb5-user
#   (if it prompts for a realm, you can leave defaults — Impacket
#    doesn't strictly need krb5.conf, but the binaries are useful)
klist          # test — 'No credentials cache found' is the expected
               # clean state before you've requested any tickets

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 4 — clone the Windows-side tooling you'll push
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
git clone https://github.com/GhostPack/Rubeus.git          # TGT/golden/PtT on Windows
git clone https://github.com/g4uss47/Invoke-Mimikatz.git   # in-memory mimikatz over PSRemoting
git clone https://github.com/Mr-Un1k0d3r/SCShell.git       # fileless lateral via service ImagePath
#   Rubeus.exe usually needs compiling (or grab a release build).

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# CLOCK SKEW — the silent Kerberos killer
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Kerberos rejects tickets if your clock is >5 min off the DC.
# If you get KRB_AP_ERR_SKEW or random PREAUTH failures:
sudo ntpdate 192.168.x.70      # or: sudo rdate -n <dc-ip>
# (timedatectl set-ntp off first if NTP keeps re-syncing to internet)
warn: Two failure modes trace straight back to this node. (1) Name resolution: if wmiexec/psexec can't find the target by FQDN, your /etc/hosts or resolv.conf is wrong — Kerberos auth is tied to the SPN which is hostname-based, so IPs won't cut it for -k workflows. (2) Clock skew: KRB_AP_ERR_SKEW or unexplained PREAUTH_FAILED with known-good creds usually means your Kali clock drifted from the DC. Sync it before debugging anything else.

choices.label: Setup done — validate where creds work (spray/check)
Run the access-check script across all hosts
Triage says local admin — PsExec / SCShell / WMI
